questions.prompt: The Butterworth filter has maximally flat passband because ζ =
questions.explanation: The damping ratio ζ = 1/√2 ≈ 0.707 makes the passband maximally flat — the variation of |H(ω)| is minimized across the passband. This is the defining characteristic of the Butterworth filter.

questions.prompt: The 2nd-order Butterworth transfer function H_B(s) is:
questions.explanation: With ζ = 1/√2, the coefficient 2ζω_n becomes 2·(1/√2)·ω_n = √2·ω_n. So H_B(s) = ω_n²/(s² + √2ω_ns + ω_n²).

questions.prompt: The magnitude response of an Nth-order Butterworth filter is:
questions.explanation: The general formula |H_B(ω)| = 1/√(1 + (ω/ω_n)^{2N}) gives maximally flat passband. The exponent is 2N (not N), which is why a 2nd-order Butterworth has (ω/ωn)⁴ in the denominator.

questions.prompt: At ω = ω_n, the Butterworth magnitude |H_B(ω_n)| equals:
questions.explanation: |H_B(ω_n)| = 1/√(1 + 1) = 1/√2. This confirms that ω_n is the 3 dB cutoff frequency — the half-power point.

questions.prompt: The poles of a 2nd-order Butterworth filter lie at:
questions.explanation: Solving s² + √2ω_ns + ω_n² = 0 gives s = (−√2ω_n ± j√2ω_n)/2 = −ω_n/√2 ± jω_n/√2. These poles are at 45° from the negative real axis, on a circle of radius ω_n.

questions.prompt: For an N-pole Butterworth filter, the angular spacing between adjacent poles is:
questions.explanation: The N poles are evenly distributed over the left-half semicircle (180°), so the angular spacing between adjacent poles is 180°/N.

questions.prompt: If N is odd in the Butterworth filter, then:
options: All poles are complex conjugate pairs
One pole lies on the negative real axis
There are no poles in the LHP
questions.correctAnswer: One pole lies on the negative real axis
questions.explanation: For odd N, one of the equally-spaced poles falls exactly on the negative real axis (at angle 180° from positive real). All other poles come in complex conjugate pairs.

questions.prompt: To scale a normalised Butterworth (ω_c = 1) to cutoff ω_c, you substitute:
questions.explanation: Frequency scaling: replace s with s/ω_c (equivalently, ω with ω/ω_c). This shifts the cutoff from 1 rad/s to ω_c rad/s while preserving the Butterworth shape.

questions.prompt: The normalised 1st-order Butterworth transfer function (ω_c = 1 rad/s) is:
questions.explanation: The simplest Butterworth: one pole at s = −1. H₁(s) = 1/(s + 1). At ω = 1: |H| = 1/√(1+1) = 1/√2 ✓ This is a first-order low-pass filter.

questions.prompt: What does 'maximally flat' mean for the Butterworth filter?
options: Zero ripple in the passband — the flattest possible response
questions.correctAnswer: Zero ripple in the passband — the flattest possible response
questions.explanation: 'Maximally flat' means the passband has the flattest possible magnitude response — as many derivatives of |H(ω)|² as possible are zero at ω = 0. There are no ripples or oscillations in the passband.

questions.prompt: For a 2nd-order Butterworth scaled to ω_c = 1000 rad/s, H_B(s) =
Compared to 2nd-order Butterworth, a 3rd-order Butterworth has:
options: Sharper transition and more poles on the semicircle
questions.correctAnswer: Sharper transition and more poles on the semicircle
questions.explanation: Higher order N means steeper rolloff (sharper transition from passband to stopband). It requires more poles, all placed on the left-half semicircle with spacing 180°/3 = 60°. The 3rd-order filter has one real pole plus one conjugate pair.
